coredata.dc:title: Chapter 57 Engineered Nanosensors Based on Molecular Imprinting Technology
coredata.prism:publicationName: Handbook of Nanomaterials for Industrial Applications
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2018 Elsevier Inc. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Keçili, Rüstem
Büyüktiryaki, Sibel
Hussain, Chaudhery Mustansar
coredata.dc:description: Natural antibody-based biosensors have some disadvantages such as low stability and high production cost. Therefore, innovative engineered materials with higher selectivity and sensitivity are needed for the recognition of the desired compound. Molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs), also called “plastic antibodies,” are a kind of materials having 3D cavities (shape of the target compound), binding groups, and exhibit high binding affinity and selectivity toward the desired compound. MIPs as plastic antibodies have gained great attention in biosensor applications in the past decades since they are stable, cheap, robust, and selective. This chapter aims to summarize and highlight the recent progresses in the field of nanobiosensor platforms based on molecular imprinting technique.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs)
nanosensors
nanoparticles
plastic antibodies
molecular recognition

ce:figure.ce:label: Figure 57.1
ce:figure.ce:source: Reproduced with permission from J. Płotka-Wasylka, N. Szczepanska, M. de la Guardia, J. Namiesnik, Miniaturized solid-phase extraction techniques, Trends Anal. Chem. 73 (2015) 19–38.

ce:figure.ce:label: Figure 57.2

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic depiction of the preparation of MWCNT/MIP-based electrochemical nanosensor toward dodecyl gallate. MWCNT, multiwalled carbon nanotube; MIP, molecularly imprinted polymer.

ce:figure.ce:source: Reproduced with permission from M.M. Pedroso, M.V. Foguel, D.H.S. Silva, M.P.T. Sotomayor, H. Yamanaka, Electrochemical sensor for dodecyl gallate determination based on electropolymerized molecularly imprinted polymer, Sens. Actuators, B 253 (2017) 180–186.

ce:figure.ce:label: Figure 57.3

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic depiction of the preparation of GN/MIP-based electrochemical sensor toward IDP. MIP, molecularly imprinted polymer; IDP, imidacloprid.

ce:figure.ce:source: Reproduced with permission from M. Zhang, H.T. Zhao, T.J. Xie, X. Yang, A.J. Dong, H. Zhang, et al., Molecularly imprinted polymer on graphene surface for selective and sensitive electrochemical sensing imidacloprid, Sens. Actuators, B 252 (2017) 991–1002.

ce:figure.ce:label: Figure 57.4

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic depiction of the preparation of MIP-based electrochemical sensor. MIP, molecularly imprinted polymer.

ce:figure.ce:source: Reproduced with permission from H. Rao, X. Zhao, X. Liu, J. Zhong, Z. Zhang, P. Zou, et al., A novel molecularly imprinted electrochemical sensor based on graphene quantum dots coated on hollow nickel nanospheres with high sensitivity and selectivity for the rapid determination of bisphenol S, Biosens. Bioelectron. 100 (2018) 341–347.

ce:figure.ce:label: Figure 57.5

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation of the preparation of MIP film-coated QCM sensor toward HSA. HSA, human serum albumin; MIP, molecularly imprinted polymer; QCM, quartz crystal microbalance.

ce:figure.ce:source: Reproduced with permission from X.-T. Ma, X.-W. He, W.-Y. Li, Y.-K. Zhang, Epitope molecularly imprinted polymer coated quartz crystalmicrobalance sensor for the determination of human serum albumin, Sens. Actuators, B 246 (2017) 879–886.

ce:figure.ce:label: Figure 57.6

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation of the preparation of MIP film-coated QCM sensor toward PC. MIP, molecularly imprinted polymer; QCM, quartz crystal microbalance.

ce:figure.ce:source: Reproduced with permission from M. Bakhshpour, E. Özgür, N. Bereli, A. Denizli, Microcontact imprinted quartz crystal microbalance nanosensor for protein C recognition, Colloid. Surf. B 151 (2017) 264–270.

ce:figure.ce:label: Figure 57.7

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Preparation of MIP-based Au/Ag nanosensor for DPA. MIP, molecularly imprinted polymer; DPA, dipicolinic acid.

ce:figure.ce:source: Reproduced with permission from A. Gültekin, A. Ersöz, N.Y. Sarıözlü, A. Denizli, R. Say, Nanosensors having dipicolinic acid imprinted nanoshell for Bacillus cereus spores detection, J. Nanopart. Res. 12 (6) (2010) 2069–2079.

ce:figure.ce:label: Figure 57.8

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic depiction of the preparation of FeSe QDs/MIP-based fluorescence nanosensor toward CYF. QD, quantum dot; MIP, molecularly imprinted polymer; CYF, cyfluthrin.

ce:figure.ce:source: Reproduced with permission from X. Li, H.-F. Jiao, X.-Z. Shi, A. Sun, X. Wang, J. Chai, et al., Development and application of a novel fluorescent nanosensor based on FeSe quantum dots embedded silica molecularly imprinted polymer for the rapid optosensing of cyfluthrin, Biosens. Bioelectron. 99 (2018) 268–273.

ce:figure.ce:label: Figure 57.9

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic depiction of the preparation of MIP-based QCM nanosensor toward atrazine. MIP, molecularly imprinted polymer; QCM, quartz crystal microbalance.

ce:figure.ce:source: Reproduced with permission from E. Yılmaz, E. Özgür, N. Bereli, D. Türkmen, A. Denizli, Plastic antibody based surface plasmon resonance nanosensors for selective atrazine detection, Mater. Sci. Eng. C 73 (2017) 603–610.

ce:figure.ce:label: Figure 57.10

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation of MIP gel-based SPR sensor having gold nanoparticles. MIP, molecularly imprinted polymer.

ce:figure.ce:source: Reproduced with permission from J. Matsui, K. Akamatsu, N. Hara, D. Miyoshi, H. Nawafune, K. Tamaki, et al., SPR sensor chip for detection of small molecules using molecularly imprinted polymer with embedded gold nanoparticles, Anal. Chem. 77 (2005) 4282–4285.

ce:figure.ce:label: Figure 57.11

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic depiction of SPR sensor coated with ultrathin MIP film: (1) gold nanolayer; (2) binder PGMA; (3) MIP; (4) gold nanoparticles; (5) cavities filled with cholesterol; (6) empty cavities. PGMA, poly(glycidyl methacrylate); MIP, molecularly imprinted polymer.

ce:figure.ce:source: Reproduced with permission from I. Tokerava, I. Tokarev, S. Minko, E. Hutter, J.H. Fendler, Ultrathin molecularly imprinted polymer sensors employing enhanced transmission surface plasmon resonance spectroscopy, Chem. Commun. 31 (2006) 3343–3345.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Recent Examples of MIP-Based Nanomaterials in Sensor Applications

row.entry: Carbon paste electrode coated with MIP
Glassy carbon electrode coated with graphene/CNT/MIP, molecularly imprinted polymer composite
Carbon paste electrode coated with CNT/MIP nanoparticles
Glassy carbon electrode coated with MIP/Pd nanoparticles composite
Carbon paste electrode coated with MIP
Glassy carbon electrode coated with graphene/MIP membrane nanocomposite
Glassy carbon electrode coated with MIP/Au nanoparticles
Glassy carbon electrode coated with CNT/MIP/Pt nanoparticles
Carbon electrode coated with graphene/MIP/Ni nanoparticles
Tap water, rain and lake water
Carbon electrode coated with graphene/MIP/Ag nanoparticles
Carbon paste electrode coated with ion imprinted nanoparticles
Glassy carbon electrode coated with graphene/prussian blue/MIP composite
Indium tin oxide electrode coated with MIP film
Carbon electrode coated with CNT/Chitosan/MIP nanoparticles
Gold disk electrode coated with MIP/Chitosan composite
Pencil graphite electrode coated with CNT/MIP composite
Gold electrode coated with MIP film
Gold electrode coated with MIP film
QCM sensor surface coated with MIP film having AAm as the functional monomer
QCM sensor surface coated with MIP film having 1,3,5-trisacrylamide-2,4,6-triazine as the functional monomer
QCM sensor surface coated with MIP film having MAA as the functional monomer
QCM sensor surface coated with MIP film having MAA as the functional monomer
QCM sensor surface having MIP membrane/Au nanoparticles/mesoporous carbon
QCM sensor surface coated with MIP/poly(o-aminothiophenol) membrane/Au nanoparticles composite
QCM sensor surface coated with MIP film having poly(amidoamine) dendrimer and MAA as the functional monomer
QCM sensor surface coated with MIP film having MAA as the functional monomer
QCM sensor surface coated with MIP film having MAAP-Eu(III) metal-chelate monomer
Chemiluminescent Fe3O4@SiO2 magnetic nanoparticles coated with MIP layer
SPR sensor surface coated with MIP layer having MAA as the functional monomer
SPR sensor surface coated with MIP layer having MAA as the functional monomer
ZnS QDs doped with Mn/MIP composite
ZnO nanorods coated with molecularly imprinted poly(ethylene-co-vinylalcohol)
Magnetic nanoparticles coated with MIP layer having MAA as the functional monomer
SPR sensor surface coated with MIP layer having MAA as the functional monomer
Graphene QDs coated with MIP layer
CdSe/ZnS QDs coated with MIP layer
Saliva, human urine, and chicken egg white
CdTe QDs coated with MIP layer
MIP/carbon dots functionalized with amino groups
Magnetic MIP particles having MAA as the functional monomer

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: MIP, molecularly imprinted polymer; CNT, carbon nanotube; QCM, quartz crystal microbalance; QD, quantum dot; AAm, acrylamide; MAAP-Eu(III), Methacryloylamido antipyrine-Europium (III).

chapter.ce:label: Chapter 57
chapter.ce:title: Engineered Nanosensors Based on Molecular Imprinting Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Medical Services and Techniques, Yunus Emre Vocational School of Health Services, Anadolu University, Eskişehir, Turkey

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Medical Services and Techniques, Yunus Emre Vocational School of Health Services, Anadolu University

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry and Environmental Science, New Jersey Institute of Technology, University Heights, Newark, NJ, United States

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry and Environmental Science, New Jersey Institute of Technology, University Heights

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Natural antibody-based biosensors have some disadvantages such as low stability and high production cost. Therefore, innovative engineered materials with higher selectivity and sensitivity are needed for the recognition of the desired compound. Molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs), also called “plastic antibodies,” are a kind of materials having 3D cavities (shape of the target compound), binding groups, and exhibit high binding affinity and selectivity toward the desired compound. MIPs as plastic antibodies have gained great attention in biosensor applications in the past decades since they are stable, cheap, robust, and selective.
This chapter aims to summarize and highlight the recent progresses in the field of nanobiosensor platforms based on molecular imprinting technique.

ce:section.ce:para: Molecular recognition is a crucial phenomena for living organisms. It is a specific interaction between a guest compound and a complementary host compound that forms a host–guest structure, such as an enzyme–substrate complex. Interactions between biomolecules such as DNA, enzyme, protein, antibody, and antigen are carried out via noncovalent interactions, such as van der Waals interactions, electrostatic interactions, hydrophobic interactions, and hydrogen bonding [1]. This molecular recognition phenomena is widely applied for the design and construction of biosensor platforms. Natural antibodies are used as recognition units in biosensors due to their excellent sensitivity and selectivity toward the desired compound. In spite of their unique properties, natural antibodies have some disadvantages such as high production cost and low stability.

Molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs), also known as “plastic antibodies or plastibodies,” are tailor-made artificial receptors that possess specific recognition sites with high affinity and selectivity toward the target compound. Thus, MIPs are commonly used in many applications where selective binding phenomena is important such as biosensors, biomimetic catalysis, and solid phase extraction [2–21]. A general procedure for the preparation of MIPs involves three steps as schematically demonstrated in Fig. 57.1
[22].
1.
Self-assembly of appropriate functional monomers around the template compound via covalent or noncovalent interactions.
2.
Polymerization in the presence of a cross-linker and initiator.
3.
Removal of the template compound from the polymeric structure.
Since the prepared highly cross-linked MIPs exhibit high selectivity toward the target compound (template), they can efficiently be used as recognition units in the preparation of biosensors.

MIP-based sensors are divided into three categories in the literature. These are electrochemical, spectroscopic, and piezoelectric sensors. Different examples of MIP-based sensors are given in the following.

A typical electrochemical sensor has three components. These are
1.
Recognition component that specifically binds target analyte.
2.
Transducer where specific reactions occur and signal is produced.
3.
Electronic component that converts the obtained signal to response.

In electrochemical detection, the reaction generally leads to a change of current (amperometric), potential (potentiometric), or conductivity (conductometric) [23]. Selectivity and sensitivity are crucial parameters for electrochemical sensors. Surface modification of electrodes in electrochemical sensors by immobilization of recognition components is an efficient approach to obtain efficient binding of the target compound with good selectivity and good response. The surface modification of electrodes in the design and preparation of electrochemical sensors was first been reported by Itaya and Bard in 1978 [24]. Since then, a lot of studies on the design and development of electrochemical sensors in different application areas have been published.

In a study reported by Alizadeh et al., an electrochemical sensor based on MIP was developed for the detection of 2,4,6-trinitrotoluene (TNT) in natural water and soil samples [25]. For this purpose, target compound TNT (template) was polymerized on the surface of a carbon paste electrode. Polymerization was carried out in the presence of metharcylic acid (MAA) and etylene glycol dimethacrylate (EGDMA) as the functional monomer and cross-linker. The efficiency of the prepared MIP-carbon paste composite electrode toward TNT was tested applying the following steps:
1.
Extraction of TNT on the surface of MIP-carbon paste composite electrode.
2.
Desorption of TNT from the electrode surface.
3.
Voltammetric detection of TNT.

The results confirmed that the prepared composite electrode coated with MIP film has high affinity and selectivity toward TNT in the presence of potentially interfering species such as phenol, aniline, p-nitrophenol, benzoic acid, and nitrobenzene. The detection limit for TNT was 1.5×10−9 M.

Gupta and Goyal prepared a new graphene/MIP-based composite sensor platform for the selective detection of melatonin in biological samples [26]. In their study, MIP film was prepared on the surface of glassy carbon electrode (GCE) by copolymerization of 4-amino-3-hydroxy-1-naphthalenesulfonic acid and melamine in the presence of template compound melatonin. The optimization studies for MIP film preparation was performed by changing the parameters such as monomer/template ratio and time. After characterization of the prepared composite electrochemical sensor for melatonin by scanning electron microscope (SEM) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), the binding performance of the sensor toward melatonin was carried out by using square wave voltammetry and cyclic voltammetry (CV). The obtained results showed that determination of melatonin in plasma samples was successfully achieved and the detection limit was determined as 0.006µM.

Pedroso et al. developed a MIP-based electrochemical nanosensor for the selective recognition of dodecyl gallate [27]. In their study, GCE was conjugated with functionalized multiwalled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs) and MIP film was synthesized by electrochemical polymerization using o-phenylenediamine as the functional monomer. After extraction of the template compound, the prepared MWCNT/MIP-based electrochemical nanosensor was used for the selective detection of dodecyl gallate. The preparation of the MWCNT/MIP-based electrochemical nanosensor is schematically demonstrated in Fig. 57.2 . The recognition performance of the prepared electrochemical nanosensor toward dodecyl gallate was investigated by using square wave voltammetry. The experimental results indicated that the prepared electrochemical nanosensor showed high selectivity and affinity toward dodecyl gallate in the presence of other competing compounds such as N-benzyl-2-nitro-1H-imidazole-1-acetamide and epigallocatechin gallate. The detection limit was calculated as 0.22×10−9 M.

A carbon nanotube (CNT)/graphene (GN)/MIP-based composite electrochemical sensor for selective recognition of bovine serum albumin (BSA) was prepared by Chen et al. [28]. For this purpose, carbon electrode (CE) was modified with GP in the first step. Then, CNT was prepared on the surface of modified CE with GN. In the final step, MIP membrane was synthesized on the CNT/GN/CE by electrodeposition of aniline in the presence of template BSA. The prepared composite sensor was tested for selective detection of BSA in human serum and the obtained results showed that the composite sensor displayed high binding behavior toward BSA. The detection limit was found to be as 6.2×10−11 g/mL.

Wang et al. developed an ZnO nanotubes/MIP-based composite electrochemical sensor for selective detection of dopamine in urine samples [29]. In the first step, electrodeposition of ZnO nanotubes was carried out on the surface of tin oxide glass doped with fluorine. After an etching step using potassium hydroxide, polypyrrole and dopamine were electropolymerized on the outer and inner surface of the ZnO nanotubes. Then, the template compound dopamine was removed from the polymeric structure. The obtained ZnO nanotubes/MIP-based nanosensor was successfully used for the selective detection of dopamine in urine samples and the results showed that the response of the prepared nanosensor toward dopamine was linear in the concentration ranges of 0.02–5 and 10–800µM.

In another interesting study reported by Smolinska-Kempisty et al., a novel MIP nanoparticles-based potentiometric sensor was prepared for the selective recognition of cocaine in blood serum samples [30]. For this purpose, MIP nanoparticles were prepared by polymerization of the functional monomer acrylamide (AAm) and cross-linker N,N′-methylenebisacrylamide in the presence of template compound benzoylecgonine, which is the main metabolite of cocaine. Then, the prepared nanoparticles were immobilized in the poly(vinyl chloride) membranes. The obtained results showed that the prepared electrochemical nanosensor exhibited excellent affinity and linear response toward cocaine in the concentration range from 1nM to 1mM.

Yola et al. reported the preparation of polyoxometalate/carbon nitride nanotubes nanocomposite-MIP-based electrochemical sensor for the determination of bilirubin in human plasma samples [31]. First, an ultrathin graphitic carbon nitride (utg-C3N4) was functionalized with polyoxometalate (H3PW12O40), then GCE was functionalized with utg-C3N4/H3PW12O40 composite and lastly electropolymerization of pyrrole was done by CV. The prepared nanocomposite was characterized by SEM, transmission electron microscope (TEM), energy dispersive x-ray, and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy. The detection limit was found to be as 3.0×10−13 M. Good recoveries were attained and the obtained results showed that determination of bilirubin in human plasma samples was successfully achieved.

Reddy et al. reported the preparation of molecularly imprinted hydrogel-based electrochemical sensor toward hemoglobin [32]. MIP film was synthesized on the surface of GCE by using functional monomer AAm in the presence of template hemoglobin. The obtained results confirmed that the prepared composite nanosensor showed high selectivity for hemoglobin.

Zhang et al. developed an electrochemical sensor toward imidacloprid (IDP) which is used as an insecticide [33]. In this study, MIP film layer was synthesized on the surface of GN sheets using p-vinylbenzoic acid as the functional monomer. Then, GCE was coated with GN/MIP composite material (Fig. 57.3 ). The prepared GN/MIP-based electrochemical sensor was successfully applied for the selective detection of IDP in rice samples. The response of the prepared electrochemical sensor was linear in the concentration range of 0.5–15µM and the detection limit was calculated as 0.10µM.

Rao et al. developed a new MIP-based electrochemical sensor system for the selective detection of bisphenol S (BPS) in mineral water samples [34]. For the preparation of electrochemical sensor system, GCE was modified with graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and then coated on hollow nickel nanospheres (hNiNS). Pyrrole was used as the functional monomer and electropolymerization was performed by CV. Fig. 57.4 shows the schematic depiction of the preparation of MIP-based electrochemical sensor toward BPS. The prepared electrochemical sensor was characterized by SEM, energy dispersive spectrometry, CV, and differential pulse voltammetry. The results obtained from the experiments showed that modification of GQDs and hNiNS onto the GCE increased the sensor sensitivity and the response toward BPS, because they amplified the active area and electron-transport ability. The linear concentration range of the sensor response was 0.1–50µM and detection limit was found to be as 0.03µM. The results obtained from the selectivity experiments showed that prepared MIP-based electrochemical sensor exhibited a great selectivity toward phosphate buffer saline (PBS) in the presence of phenol, hydroquinone, p-nitrophenol, and sulfanilic acid. Selectivity experiments, real sample analyses, reproducibility and stability tests proved that the developed electrochemical sensor system can also successfully be applied for the selective detection of PBS in the plastic samples.

In another study reported by Prasad et al., a composite electrochemical sensor composed of MIP film and MWCNTs was prepared for the detection of l-histidine [35]. For the preparation of MIP film selective to l-histidine, 2-acryl amidoethyldihydrogen phosphate and EGDMA were used as the functional monomer and cross-linker, respectively. The functional monomer was interacted with Cu(II) to obtain metal-chelate functional monomer. Then, polymerization was carried out in the presence of Cu(II)-functional monomer–template complex. The prepared MIP-based electrochemical sensor showed enantioselectivity toward l-histidine and the detection limit was found to be 1.980ng/mL. However, cross-reactivity studies of the prepared sensor for potentially interfering compounds in the sample such as d-histidine, l- and d-tryptophan, l-phenylalanine, l-tyrosine, l-alanine, l-glycine, l-methionine, l-proline, dopamine, creatinine, urea, uric acid, l-glutamic acid, and l-ascorbic acid were also performed. The experimental results confirmed that the prepared nanosensor displayed very low response for these compounds.

Wang et al. reported the preparation of CdS quantum dot (QD)/graphene/MIP-based electrochemical sensor for selective recognition of 4-aminophenol in water samples [36]. In their study, fluorine-doped tin oxide electrode was modified with CdS QDs and GN. Then, a MIP film selective to target compound 4-aminophenol was prepared by electropolymerization. The results showed that the prepared nanosensor specifically binds the target compound 4-aminophenol. The response of the sensor was linear in the concentration range of 5.0×10−8 to 3.5×10−6 M and the detection limit was calculated as 2.3×10−8 M.

Quartz crystal microbalance (QCM) is an analytical tool sensitive to mass changes on the surface. Many studies on different applications of QCM sensors were reported in the literature and some examples are briefly described in the following.

Liu et al. developed MIP-based QCM sensors for the selective recognition of benzaldehyde, hexanal, and nonanal [37]. For this purpose, benzoic acid, hexanoic acid, and nonanoic acid were used as dummy templates for the preparation MIP layer on the sensor surface. 13 different MIPs were prepared by sol–gel technique and coated on the sensor surface by spin-coating. The optimization studies were also carried out and the prepared sensor systems were applied for the recognition of three aldehydes, benzaldehyde, hexanal, and nonanal. The experimental results indicated that the prepared MIP film-coated QCM sensor having five channels exhibited high affinity toward target aldehydes.

The research group of Denizli prepared a MIP film-coated QCM sensor for the recognition of bilirubin in human plasma samples [38]. For this purpose, MIP film synthesis was carried out on the gold electrode surface by polymerization of the functional monomers 2-hydroxyethyl methacrylate (HEMA), methacryloylamido tryptophan, and cross-linker EGDMA in the presence of template compound bilirubin. The prepared MIP film-coated QCM sensor was successfully applied for the recognition of bilirubin in human plasma samples. The prepared QCM sensor exhibited high selectivity toward bilirubin in the presence of other competing compounds such as cholesterol and estradiol. The detection limit was found to be 0.45µg/mL.

Ersöz et al. developed a 8-hydroxy-2-deoxyguanosine (8-OHdG) imprinted film coated-QCM sensor for the selective recognition of 8-OHdG in human serum [39]. MIP layer on the QCM sensor surface was synthesized by photograft surface polymerization of methacryloylamido antipyrine-iron as the metal chelate functional monomer, N-N′-methylenebisacrylamide as the cross-linker, and template 8-OHdG. The obtained results from the experiments on the determination of 8-OHdG level in human serum by using the prepared sensor system confirmed that the prepared MIP film-coated QCM sensor exhibited high selectivity toward 8-OHdG and the affinity constant was determined as 78,760M−1.

In another important study, Ma et al. prepared a MIP film-coated QCM sensor for the selective detection of human serum albumin (HSA) in human serum samples [40]. In their study, the functional monomer zinc acrylate and cross-linker EGDMA were used for the preparation of MIP film. The schematic representation of the preparation of MIP film-coated QCM sensor toward HSA is given in Fig. 57.5 . The obtained results showed that the prepared MIP film-coated QCM sensor displayed excellent binding performance toward the target protein HSA with a maximum binding capacity of 166mg/g. The detection limit was found to be 0.026µg/mL.

Lu et al. prepared a QCM sensor having selective MIP layer toward glycoprotein 41 (gp41) which is a subunit of the envelope protein of HIV-1 virus [41]. For this purpose, they used dopamine as the functional monomer and an artificial peptide having 35 amino acid as the template. After polymerization of the functional monomer in the presence of the template on the QCM sensor surface, the prepared MIP-based QCM sensor was applied for the detection of gp41 in urine samples. The obtained results confirmed that the prepared MIP-based QCM sensor showed great binding performance and selectivity toward the target protein gp41. The detection limit was calculated as 2ng/mL which is a comparable value to the reported enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay results in the literature.

In a study reported by Vergara et al. [42], QCM surface was coated with polythiophene film by using electrochemically molecularly imprinted technique for the detection of pinacolyl methylphosphonate (PMP) which is an analog of a chemical nerve agent. For this purpose, MIP film was prepared on the sensor surface by using CV. Attenuated total reflection infrared spectroscopy, ellipsometry, static water contact angle goniometry, atomic force microscopy (AFM), and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy were applied for the characterization of the prepared MIP film-coated QCM sensor. The selectivity of the prepared QCM sensor toward PMP was investigated using dimethyl methylphosphonate and phenyl phosphonic acid as competing compounds. The prepared MIP film-coated QCM sensor showed high sensitivity and selectivity toward PMP. The linear concentration range of the sensor response was 60–200µM. The detection limit and quantification limit for PMP were 44.95 and 149.85µM, respectively.

In another important study reported by the research group of Denizli [43], a MIP film-coated QCM sensor was developed for the selective recognition of protein C (PC) in human serum. Polymeric nanofilm on the surface of QCM sensor was prepared by using microcontact imprinting technique. For this purpose, glass slides were modificated with (3-aminopropyl)triethoxysilane (APTES) and glutaraldehyde. Then, PC was immobilized on the surface of a functionalized glass slide. N-methacryloyl l-histidinemethylester-Cu(II) [MAH-Cu(II)] was used as the metal-chelate functional monomer. A mixture of the functional monomer MAH-Cu(II), cross-linker EGDMA and initiator 2,2′-azoisobutyronitrile was dropped on the surface of QCM chip functionalized with allyl mercaptan. UV polymerization was carried out between PC immobilized on the surface of glass side and functionalized-QCM chip surface. After polymerization, the PC immobilized-glass slide was removed from the surface of QCM chip. The schematic representation of the preparation of QCM sensor coated with MIP film toward PC is shown in Fig. 57.6 . The prepared MIP-based QCM nanosensor was characterized by AFM, ellipsometry, contact angle measurements, and Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) studies. The obtained results showed that microcontact imprinting technique is an efficient approach for the preparation of QCM nanosensor toward PC. The prepared MIP film-coated QCM nanosensor displayed high binding affinity and selectivity toward PC in the presence of other competing proteins such as fibrinogen, HSA, and hemoglobin. The limit of detection was obtained as 0.01µg/mL.

MIP-based spectroscopic sensors can be divided into three categories. These are MIP-based fluorescence sensors, MIP-based chemiluminescence sensors, and MIP-based Surface plasmon resonance (SPR) sensors. In the fluorescence based molecular recognition of the target compound, fluorescence functional monomers are used for the preparation of MIP-based sensor [44]. When the target compound binds to the sensor, fluorescence intensity increases or decreases depends on the sensor design.

The group of Say prepared gold–silver nanoclusters coated with dipicolinic acid (DPA) imprinted nanoshell for selective recognition of Bacillus cereus spores [45]. For this purpose, DPA and methacryloyl imidodiacetic acid-chromium(III) were used as the template and metal-chelate functional monomer, respectively. The obtained results from fluorescence measurements indicated that the fluorescence nanosensor exhibited high recognition behavior toward DPA. Langmuir and Scatchard binding isotherms were applied to describe binding behavior of the prepared nanosensors toward target DPA. K affinity values were 18×106 and 9×106 mol/L, respectively.

The same research group reported the preparation of gold–silver nanoclusters with MIP nanoshell using functional monomer methacryloylamido antipyrine-terbium for selective recognition of DPA [46]. A schematic depiction of the prepared gold–silver nanoclusters having DPA imprinted nanoshell is shown in Fig. 57.7 . The binding performance of the prepared nanosensor toward DPA was investigated by fluorescence spectroscopy. The experimental results confirmed that the prepared MIP-based nanosensor exhibited high affinity for DPA. K affinity values determined by Langmuir and Scatchard binding isotherms were 1.43×104 and 9.1×106 mol/L, respectively.

In another interesting reported study [47], highly hydrophilic and biocompatible MIP-based QD fluorescence sensors for the detection of phycocyanin and bovine hemoglobin (BHb) in water and urine samples were developed by Wang et al. For this purpose, CdTe QDs were functionalized with thioglycolic acid and glutathione. Dopamine provides biocompatible self-polymerization on the QD surface and therefore it was used as the cross-linker. The prepared fluorescent nanosensors were characterized by FT-IR and TEM. The results obtained from the fluorescence measurements showed that the fluorescence intensity of QDs decreased depending on the concentration of PC and BHb. The limit of detection and imprinting factor for the template compound PC were found to be 0.075 and 7.3µM, respectively. On the other hand, the detection limit and imprinting factor for BHb were found to be 0.069 and 4.2µM, respectively.

In another interesting work, Li et al. developed a new fluorescence nanosensor for the detection of cyfluthrin (CYF) in fish and sediment samples [48]. In their study, FeSe QDs were modified with APTES and MIP layer was prepared on the surface of QDs by using functional monomer MAA and cross-linker EGDMA. Fig. 57.8 shows the schematic representation of the preparation of FeSe QDs/MIP-based fluorescence nanosensor toward CYF. The obtained results showed that the prepared fluorescence nanosensor can successfully be used for the detection of CYF in fish and sediment samples. The selectivity experiments were also performed by using other competing compounds, such as deltamethrin, bifenthrin, fenvalerate, and cypermethrin, which are analogs of CYF. The prepared FeSe QDs/MIP-based fluorescence nanosensor displayed excellent affinity, linearity, and selectivity toward target compound CYF. The response of the prepared fluorescence nanosensor was linear in the concentration range of 0.01–0.2mg/L and the limit of detection values were obtained as 1.0 and 1.3µg/kg for CYF in fish and sediment samples, respectively.

Sergeyeva et al. prepared a molecularly imprinted membrane-based fluorescence nanosensor for the selective detection of aflatoxin B1 in wastewater samples [49]. First, LEAPFROG algorithm was used for the investigation of the interaction between functional monomers and target compound aflatoxin B1. Allylamine, AAm, 2-acrylamido-2-methyl-1-propansulfonic acid, 2-(diethylamino)ethylmethacrylate, and N,N′-methylenebisacrylamide were tested to determine appropriate functional monomer. The obtained results from the computational modeling showed that all functional monomers exhibited high binding energy toward aflatoxin B1. In this study, ethyl-2-oxocyclopentanecarboxylate was used as a dummy template since its structure is close to the aflatoxin B1 and it is also nontoxic. For the preparation of a mechanically stable membrane, tri(ethyleneglycol)dimethacrylate, oligourethaneacrylate, and dimethylformamide were used as the cross-linker, modifier-elasticator, and porogenic solvent, respectively. The selectivity studies of the prepared MIP membrane toward aflatoxin B1 was carried out in the presence of aflatoxin B2, aflatoxin G2, and ochratoxin A. The results confirmed that the prepared nanostructured MIP membrane showed excellent selectivity toward aflatoxin B1 when AAm was used as the functional monomer. The detection limit was found to be as 14ng/mL.

Chemiluminescence is another efficient approach that is used for the investigation of the recognition performance of MIP-based spectroscopic sensors. In this approach, a chemiluminescence system is chosen and selective MIPs are integrated to this system. When a target compound binds to the MIP-based sensor, chemiluminescence emission is generated. The amount of the emission depends on the amount of bound target compound to the sensor surface.

Chullasat et al. developed a QDs-based MIP nanosensor for the detection of amoxicillin in food samples [6]. In their study, CdTe QDs were coated with a MIP shell by sol–gel technique using the functional monomer 3-aminopropylethoxysilane and cross-linker tetraethoxysilane. The prepared QDs-MIP nanosensor was successfully applied for the selective detection of amoxicillin in honey, egg, and milk samples. The obtained results showed that the quenching of photoluminescence intensity of QDs-MIP nanosensor was higher than the response of the nonimprinted nanosensor. The limit of detection was calculated as 0.14µg/L.

In a study performed by Zor et al., a magnetic silica/GQDs/MIP-based composite photoluminescent sensor for selective detection of pesticides was developed [50]. For this purpose, tributyltin (TBT) was used as the template pesticide for the preparation of the MIP-based nanosensor. The performance of the prepared sensor was tested by using fluorescence spectroscopy. Selectivity studies of the composite nanosensor toward TBT in the presence of monobutyltin and dibutyltin were also carried out. The obtained result showed that the prepared magnetic silica/GQDs/MIP-based nanosensor showed high selectivity toward TBT in water samples. The detection limits for TBT in water and seawater were found to be 12.78 and 42.56ppb, respectively.

On the other hand, SPR-based sensors are also popular recognition systems. SPR technique relies on the measurement of the changes in refractive index of thin layer on the metal surface. The recognition element on the surface of the sensor is usually gold or silver coated with thin film. Therefore, uniform film layer is synthesized on the surface of MIP-based SPR sensors.

Many studies on the applications of MIP-based SPR sensors were reported. For example, Kugimiya and Takeuchi developed SPR sensor chip coated with MIP film for selective recognition of sialic acid [51]. In their study, N,N,N-trimethylaminoethyl methacrylate and HEMA were copolymerized in the presence of cross-linker EGDMA and template compound sialic acid-p-vinylbenzene boronic acid ester. After polymerization, the template was removed from the polymeric structure by using 0.1-M HCI/MeOH (1:1). The prepared sensor showed high selectivity toward gangliosides having sialic acid.

Yılmaz et al. developed a MIP nanoparticles-coated SPR sensor system for the selective recognition of atrazine which is one of the most widely used pesticides in the world [52]. In this study, MIP nanoparticles were prepared by using functional monomers methacryloylamido aspartic acid, HEMA, and cross-linker EGDMA. The schematic representation of the preparation of SPR sensor having MIP nanoparticles toward atrazine is shown in Fig. 57.9 . The results confirmed that the prepared SPR-based nanosensor has excellent affinity and selectivity toward the target compound atrazine in the presence of other competing compounds such as amitrole and simazine. The limit of detection was obtained as 0.7134ng/mL.

In another published research by Lotierzo et al., a SPR sensor for domoic acid was prepared by grafting MIP film on the surface of a gold chip [53]. The photografting process was carried out in three steps. In the first step, the gold surface was functionalized with 2-mercaptoethanol. Then, a photoinitiator 4,4′-azobis(cyanovaleric acid) was covalently bound to the surface. In the last step, functional monomer 2-(diethylamino)ethyl methacrylate and template compound domoic acid were polymerized in the presence of cross-linker EGDMA. Grafting process control and optimization was performed by using drop shape analysis and AFM. The performance of the prepared SPR sensor toward domoic acid was tested using free domoic acid and its conjugate horseradish peroxidase. Monoclonal antibodies as natural receptors were also used in this study. The experimental results confirmed that the regeneration of the prepared MIP-based SPR sensor has a negligible effect on the binding of target compound to the sensor compared to monoclonal antibodies. The detection limit of MIP-based SPR sensor was 5µg/L and the limit of detection antibody-based SPR sensor was 1.8µg/L. On the other hand, antibodies immobilized on the surface of gold lost their activities after five regenerations while the activity of MIP-based SPR sensor decreased after 30 regenerations.

In another work [54], after modification of gold sensor surface with allyl mercaptan, acrylic acid, N-isopropyl AAm, and gold nanoparticles were polymerized in the presence of target compound dopamine. The results showed that the sensitivity of prepared MIP film-coated SPR sensor selective to dopamine greatly increased by incorporating gold nanoparticles. In this study, MIP gel on the surface of the sensor swells when dopamine molecules are bound and this swelling leads to an increase in the distance between nanoparticles and gold film on the SPR surface. This leads to a shift in the SPR angle. The obtained results showed that this approach has many advantages especially for the determination of small molecules. The prepared MIP gel-based SPR sensor having gold nanoparticles is schematically depicted in Fig. 57.10 .

Another MIP film-coated SPR sensor system for determination of cholesterol was prepared by Tokerava et al. [55]. MIP film synthesis on the surface of sensor was performed in five steps. In the first step, glass substrate was coated with 14.5±5.8-nm-thick gold film. In the second step, poly(glycidyl methacrylate) (PGMA) layer was added. In the third step, grafting of poly(2-vinylpyridin) (P2VP) through epoxy functional group of PGMA was carried out on the surface. The thickness of coated P2VP was found to be 7.6±0.8nm by using ellipsometry. Then, the MIP layer was synthesized by using cholesterol and gold nanoparticles in the presence of cross-linker. In the last step, gold nanoparticles were readsorbed onto the cross-linked polymeric film layer. The preparation of MIP-based SPR sensor is schematically shown in Fig. 57.11 .

The experimental results indicated that the developed MIP film-coated SPR sensor showed high affinity toward cholesterol. When cholesterol molecules bind to the sensor, SPR maximum absorption band shifted from 654 to 598nm.
Table 57.1 shows the recent examples of MIP-based nanomaterials in sensor applications.

The growing number of studies in which MIP-based nanomaterials were used for biosensor applications confirmed
that these special materials are promising for the selective detection of target compounds. The reported studies briefly described in this chapter highlight the recent advances in sensor platforms prepared by using nanostructured MIPs over the past years. Nanostructured materials have superior features such as large surface area, high stability, high reactivity, and versatile chemistry for the further functionalization. More binding sites are available on the surface of the nanomaterials, which is crucial for the sensor applications. MIPs in nanoscale provide a new approach for the design and fabrication of sensors with high affinity and selectivity toward target compounds in complex matrices such as food and biological samples. On the other hand, the combination of MIPs with QCM, SPR, and electrochemical techniques offer new routes to explore, design, and prepare the next generation of sensor platforms in different application areas. Although many reported applications of MIP-based nanosensors were successfully implemented, there are still some problems that need to be solved before these MIP-based nanosensors are commercially available in the future. The main challenges are preparation of nano-MIPs with uniform binding sites and development of a general synthesis protocol. The future attempts and approaches by researchers from different disciplines such as chemistry, biology, and material engineering will gradually extend the biosensor applications of MIP-based nanomaterials in the real world.
